author: @BaronSamedi1959
text:
All designs to scavenge electricity from roads have been dismal failures. Hundreds of designs and a few small implementations, but no large-scale working models.

And you know why? One cannot generate energy out of nothing.

Imagine a road where cars push down on the road surface, and through this downward force, generate electricity either mechanically or electronically (such as through the piezoelectric effect). But pushing down requires a force, which means that the car, when moving from the already pushed-down road tile to the next not yet pushed-down road tile, has to go uphill ever so slightly, and thus expends a little extra energy. So this scavenged energy is not "free" but is "stolen" from the moving car. And since generating electricity is never 100% efficient, the end result is a net waste of energy.

author: @johns.5864
text:
Another source of reciprocating motion is tree branches in the wind or breeze.  They get displaced by the wind and being like a torsional spring mass system can oscillate a bit.  Imagine a mechanism like a lawn mower pull start.  The cord is tied to the branch with some tension.  The other end is anchored in the ground or a structure.  As the branch moves back and forth the mechanism turns in one direction such that the energy is used.

The largest part that captures the energy is the branch which is free.  No massive wind turbine, water wheel, or wind turbine.

Energy is most valuable when it is needed and there is not a cheaper alternative.  Solar cells were practical in space long before mass generation because they were much cheaper than alternatives like batteries where the energy would be expensive due to getting them into orbit.

So getting back to the topic, is there a demand for energy where there are lots of suns blocking trees?  In forests you don’t have household AC.  Undeveloped forests don’t have roads to transport large machinery.

A tree energy harvester might fit in a backpack and have numerous chords allowing energy from multiple branches to be extracted.

author: @FlatterTundra9
text:
I have no background in engineering of any sort, but it appears as though the compliant/bending part of the mechanism is mostly driven by the forward and backward motion of its base, rather than its vertical or complete rotary motion. I wonder, then, if that gear could be replaced by something linear. 
Maybe you could fit that theoretical linear part inside a large boot sole that would be compressed on a step, sliding the arm back and forth and turning the output gear?

Looking at a few news articles that reported on the already-existing military technology you mentioned in the video, it seems the linearity question has been solved already. You mentioned something about a bouncing weight and watch winding mechanisms, so maybe you already know the answer as well. 

Soldiers apparently noticed an "unnatural sinking feeling" in an earlier prototype that was later improved.

I think a problem with considering walking "wasted energy" is that it's really not. If you used less muscle to take a step than normal while changing nothing else, you'd fall over.
That "sinking feeling" in those energy harvesting boots was from them effectively adding an inch or so of extra step height to every pace they took, which increased the load they had to overcome to take each step. /That/ is where the "harvested energy" comes from. Not the regular step.

Still, very cool! 
This is the first video of yours I've seen, but it will certainly NOT be the last.

author: @evilpandakillabzonattkoccu4879
text:
I think it's interesting: all of our joints move in a circular motion, yet our body uses that to create linear motion. 

Like throwing a ball or a punch....or any movement like that (swinging a golf club or baseball bat)... the energy comes from the rotation of the body, but it's used, in the end, to create linear force: a straight throw, pass or punch, a straight golf shot (or one with a more controlled flight), a straight baseball hit. 

....if you throw a ball and just let go, it goes right into the ground. of course...its following the circular motion of your arm. Thus, we let your fingers drag on the ball, inducing backspin. That negates the circular motion of our body (among other things) and we get a longer, more straight throw (that's why your coach keeps telling you to 'follow through' when throwing a ball or shooting a basketball, kids 👍). 

Just...something I always thought was curious about humans.

author: @bjamesW32
text:
This is genuine stuff.  But the practical applications are limited to things like watches.  Extremely low demand items. The no free lunch laws of thermodynamics.   Anything beyond such exceedingly small demands will be felt  by the   original mover, you, as fatigue. 

Anything that can be identified as wasted energy is game for recovery. But the human body is a pretty damn efficient machine. Recouping wasted heat or motion is a little akin to mounting a fan bladed generator to the front of your car. At first it makes sense. And it might have made small sense in the 20's.  But modern cars, like human bodies, are marvels of efficiency.  Any energy gained by the fan in front of your car is almost entirely offset by added weight and altered aerodynamics over the rest of the car body.  Actually after parasitic losses its probably even negative. 

Seiko's device cleverly harvests that barely noticeable energy "waste" you produce by your arm movement.  But it is parasitic none the less. It's just so tiny that you don't notice it.  And it is so tiny that it is evidently not even worth the tradeoff in space to incorporate some version of it into a (more than a wrist watch) high energy demand device like a cell phone. Or it would have been done by now.

author: @ericmadsen8324
text: Just note that almost any physical motion energy generation basically adds to the users energy output, e.g. It wears the user out faster. The only way energy recovery works is from converting waste energy disassociated from the direct action of the user, e.g. heat by-product. If it captures power from resisting the user motion, it's not "recovery" but "addition", as the user has to expend now more energy to achieve the same intended motion. That's why thermal wearables are promising, because heat is generally a waste by-product that is beneficial to the user to remove.

author: @joedee1863
text:
Wish we could meet up Robert and I could show you my drawings of something I saw as a boy on holiday at a seaside resort with my parents. It was a treadmill railway which went round a track and young and old could use it no problem. The riders dropped onto a wide treadle which was geared both sides to go one way regardless of which way anyone was facing. A flywheel kept the momentum going and springs under the treadle brought the bouncing riders back to their level again ready for the next jump on 
so no flexing of muscles here just stepping on and off with handrails keeping the balance. 
The thing looked a pendulum when it was in motion with very little effort from the riders.
I saw something similar used in Africa pumping water but it was a two man operation and too small. No flywheel no springs no gears.
It was a muscle flexing machine without the advantage of body weight and gravity 
I visualise a four man tread apparatus which is good for geriatrics like me 
It could generate power and have a continuous flow of riders taking turns to keep the momentum going and doing it to music can cause a lot of acceleration and exhilaration. 😅

author: @siberx4
text: While this is an interesting mechanism, all the mentioned research into "harvesting" energy from walking is a bit of a waste of time. Any energy lost while walking/hiking is an inherent inefficiency of the process, and can't be meaningfully harvested. Any extra contraption you introduce into the system will instead sap _additional_ energy from your walking motion (kind of like walking through sand), making you more tired and requiring you to exert yourself harder. Might as well just spin a hand crank generator while you walk, or better yet strap a solar panel to your bag or something.

author: @meettechniek
text:
Robert, you're talking nonsense. A task, like doing one step, is doing work. This is an amount of energy expressed in joules, not watt. Next you're multiplying a power of 10 Watt it with a number of 10000 steps, and calculate the result: 10000 watt*hours. Where did the hours come from? Think a litte bit more about the units you're using.
Next: harvesting energy from daily task like walking makes it more tiring and it is less comfortable. It's a  bad idea!
An rough estimation of that flexible footpath. Say the person weights 70 kg, the foothpad is pushed in 5 mm then the energy for one step is E=m*g*h = 70*9.8*0.005 = 3.4 J. Doing a walk of 10000 steps will generate 34 Joule without losses. A small solar panel of 150 mm * 150 mm  12 hours in and aimed at the sun will generate about the same amount of energy. Why building such a complex, vulnerable, costly and tiring mechanical foothpad when you can use something cheap and ready available solarpanels.

author: @mikeonb4c
text: I've thought about stuff like this since I was an A Level student in the 1970s. I thought about electric cars, regenerative braking etc and have been thinking about it all ever since. The idea of tapping energy off the movement of our body interests me, principally because I wonder (but can't be sure) whether our bodies are in fact more efficient machines than anything we have created.  If that is the case, and (for example) our bodies are made to work harder by walking on a floor that absorbs our working energy by yielding and itself generating energy, and if our bodies then require more calorie input because they are doing more work, and bearing in mind the energy cost of manufacturing, installing and maintaining these energy absorbing floors, then what is the real advantage they offer (if any) in energy savings.  Thinking even more widely, what are all these humans doing walking around in places where the footfall is sufficient to justify investment in energy generating pavements. For example, is it to visit shopping centres where they buy a lot of staff that itself takes energy to make and which is not strictly essential? Is it do go to workplaces where they earn employment doing work that could just as easily have been done from where they live, and without co2 generating commutes. The more you think about this, the more there is to think about, and I start to realise that whilst some of the novel energy generating technologies may be fascinating to learn about, they may be a distraction from thinking deeply about the nature of how we live and how perhaps we might live differently in ways that are still meaningful and fulfilling but that make better use of that most efficient of machines - our bodies, and our minds - to build that planet saving sustainable future we all seek.

author: @etmax1
text:
So why don't we just build a perpetual motion machine? Surely that's better? 
Here's the thing, in physics there's no free lunch, if you want to extract 14W out of a person walking then that person needs to expend that energy in their muscles and ligaments and to be able to do that without dying of hunger they need to consume an extra 14 calories worth of food. 
Now while food does grow on trees (amongst other things), we need to expend energy making fertiliser and shipping that food to the super market and going down there to buy it and then bring it home.
Then there's packaging and waste disposal etc. etc.

So what does this mean? We really need to do the calculations on this and probably need to pay the people for their effort.

So a human being is said to consume 100W at rest and if they want to extract 14W then according to chatgtp the human body probably consumes 3.5 times that so let's say 350W for 14W. 

Somehow I don't see this paying off, when the off peak rate for electricity is only 16c per kWh.

Before you say "if you're already walking..." remember that you will probably hit 500W by extracting that additional 14W.

author: @petehiggins33
text:
There's no such thing as free energy.  Generally speaking anything that enables a person's normal activity to generate electrical power will make them work harder.  And of course the same is true of vehicles.  Any system using say piezoelectric generators embedded in the road will increase the car's fuel consumption.  

Having said that there are situations where we waste energy by dissipating it such as when a walker is descending a hill.  He is dissipating his potential energy that he gained by ascending the hill, mostly in his muscles.  If a method of recovering that energy into a battery could be devised then it wouldn't cause him any extra effort.   In fact it would reduce the wear and tear on muscles and joints and make walking easier.  This is regenerative braking for people.  Of course you would have to carry the weight of the generator up the hill but you would get that energy back on the way down and the extra effort would be offset by reducing the weight of batteries you would need to carry.

author: @toocleanpappas5397
text:
I really hope you pursue this! I am a Thru-hiker and have walked about 10,000 miles and keeping electronics charged on trail is always something we struggle with. In more open places you can use solar, but on a lot of trails with tree cover it just isn't viable. My friends and I definitely have thought about something to take advantage of the energy we use walking, but haven't seen anything like this. Given that we tend to walk around 8-10 hours a day average, this really could be viable for us. 

One thing to be aware of is that the energy to lift something from your foot (Like shoes) is equivalent to carrying about 5 times that on your back. So if you have a pair of shoes that weight 1 lb vs a pair that weight 2 , you will  be expending about 5 lbs extra in energy of work. The military did some studies on this years ago. 

I mention this because if what you spend in energy over say a 5 day stretch lifting a heavy electric generator system on your shoes is more than say carrying and extra 3/4 lb battery bank, it wouldn't be much use, for us at least. Though, if you live in a city and want a bit extra exercise it could be worth it.  
Anyhow, been watching for a while and love your channel ! Keep up the good work!

author: @thetruenolan6655
text:
Way back in the late 1960s I saw an article describing a similar ratchet mechanism hooked up to a rope tied toward the top of a tall tree. As the tree swayed in the wind it produced some very high tensions and some fairly long strokes. If you matched such a "tree and ratchet" mechanism with a heavy weight driven generator (on the ground next to the tree) you might get some usable power. If you use three or four top ropes (every 120 or 90 degrees) you can get a pull on the ratchet no matter what direction the tree is blown in.

One small correction: A LOT of life uses rotary motion instead of reciprocating motion -- but we don't normally see it because the life is single celled. You know all those microscopic movies of bacteria with propulsive flagella waving snake-like behind? They aren't actually flailing, they are actually rotating corkscrews and just appear to be flailing because they rotate. The base of each screw has a tiny chemical/electric "motor" twirling it about.

Ain't life AMAZING?! And your videos are great too! Thanks?

replies.text: @matthewellisor5835 Exhibition back self winding watches are pretty common now.  Even Rolex adopted this just this year.  The unfortunate problem with this is the self winding mechanism is nearly concentric centered, so even when skeletonized, the pendulum weight makes seeing the operation impossible.  I have two watches like this, and I've poured over them to try and divine how this mechanism works, but with this video, it's obvious now.  The only not obvious part is getting the driven wheel (attached to the mainspring) nearly concentric with the pendulum wheel, but I have a pretty clear idea of how they do this in my mind's eye.  There are any number of watch servicing videos on YouTube for reference, but this large model truly pulls back the covers on how it really works.

replies.author: @toml.8210
replies.text: Older watches don't have the lever or a clutch, so it only winds when the rotor moves in the one direction, and the mainspring may break.  Seiko fixed that.

replies.author: @KenFullman
replies.text: There's also a high gear ratio between the auto winder and the mainspring, that allows this really tiny weight, to apply torque to the mainspring and then there's a clutch that prevents the auto wind mechanism to snap the spring.
